en.extract:
The lilacine amazon (Amazona lilacina), also known as the Ecuadorian red-lored amazon, is an amazon parrot native to Ecuador in South America. The International Union for Conservation of Nature classifies the lilacine amazon as critically endangered.
It is generally smaller than the closely realated red-lored amazon, with a black beak and more subdued coloring. Lilacine amazons are said to make favorable companion parrots because of their gentle, affectionate nature and agreeable temperament.


Description
The lilacine amazon is a small parrot, approximately 34 cm long when mature, with primarily green plumage. Like the red-lored amazon, it has red lores and yellow cheeks; its distinguishing features include a fully black beak, and lilac-tipped feathers on its crown.


Behavior
The lilacine amazon is known for its gentle and timid nature. In the wild it avoids confrontation with intruders, opting to seek cover in nearby foliage until danger has passed. It usually spends time in small- to medium-sized groups, with which it roosts at night; many birds pair off monogamously within the larger group, and they are most frequently seen flying in pairs.


Diet
The diet of the lilacine amazon consists primarily of fruits, nuts, berries, and seeds. Like most parrots, it possesses a powerful beak; its dexterous tongue also helps to break down and consume a diverse suite of foodstuffs.


Breeding
Sexual maturity is reached at three or four years of age. Like most parrots, the lilacine amazon is a monogamous breeder and a cavity nester. The hen will lay two to four eggs in a tree cavity and incubate them for a period of three weeks; the young fledge about two months after hatching.
As with many other parrots, the male will keep the female and chicks nourished during their time in the nest by consuming additional food and regurgitating it for them.


Distribution and habitat
The lilacine amazon is native to the tropical dry forests of western Ecuador north of the Gulf of Guayaquil, extending to Nariño in extreme south-western Colombia adjacent to the Ecuadorian border, where it intersects with the subspecies A. a. salvini.


Aviculture
Lilacine amazons have become well regarded as companion parrots, intelligent with a personality often described as gentle, affectionate, and loyal. In captivity, they enjoy the company of people, and will often form a strong bond with their owner. They are not known as exceptional talkers, but most individuals are able to learn to mimic at least a few phrases. The World Parrot Trust recommends that the lilacine amazon be housed in an enclosure with a minimum length of 3 metres.
The lilacine amazon has only recently become popular and common in the pet trade, formerly being quite rare. At least one captive breeding program has been established in Europe to increase the bird's numbers.


References


External links

Amazona lilacina - Ecuador Amazon

[[File:Amazona autumnalis lilacina -Chester Zoo -upper body-8a.jpg|right|thumb|At [[Chester Zoo]]]]
The '''lilacine amazon''' ('''''Amazona lilacina'''''), also known as the Ecuadorian red-lored amazon,<ref name="worldparrottrust">{{cite web |title=Lilacine amazon (Amazona lilacina) |url=https://www.parrots.org/encyclopedia/lilacine-amazon# |publisher=World Parrot Trust |access-date=10 March 2021}}</ref> is an [[amazon parrot]] native to [[Ecuador]] in [[South America]]. The [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] classifies the lilacine amazon as critically endangered.<ref name="iucn">{{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |date=2020 |title=''Amazona lilacina'' |volume=2020 |article-number=e.T22728296A181432250 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2020-3.RLTS.T22728296A181432250.en |access-date=19 November 2021}}</ref>

It is generally smaller than the closely realated red-lored amazon, with a black [[beak]] and more subdued coloring.<ref name="opa">{{cite web|url=http://proaviculture.com/amazons.htm|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040216153458/http://www.proaviculture.com/amazons.htm|url-status=usurped|archive-date=February 16, 2004|title=Amazon Parrots, Part 1|access-date=10 November 2008|publisher=Organization of Professional Aviculturists}}</ref><ref name="brough">{{cite web|url=http://animal-world.com/encyclo/birds/amazons/lilacineamazon.php|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060323065131/http://animal-world.com/encyclo/birds/amazons/lilacineamazon.php|archive-date=March 23, 2006|title=Lilacine Amazon|access-date=10 November 2008|last=Brough|first=Clarice|publisher=Animal-World}}</ref> Lilacine amazons are said to make favorable [[companion parrot]]s because of their gentle, affectionate nature and agreeable temperament.<ref name="brough" />

== Description ==
The lilacine amazon is a small parrot, approximately 34 cm long when mature, with primarily green plumage. Like the red-lored amazon, it has red lores and yellow cheeks; its distinguishing features include a fully black beak, and lilac-tipped feathers on its crown.<ref name="brough" /><ref name="juniper">{{cite book|last=Juniper|first=Tony|author2=Mike Parr |author3=Kim Franklin |title=Parrots: A Guide to Parrots of the World|year=1998|publisher=[[Yale University Press]]}}</ref>

== Behavior ==
The lilacine amazon is known for its gentle and timid nature. In the wild it avoids confrontation with intruders, opting to seek cover in nearby foliage until danger has passed. It usually spends time in small- to medium-sized groups, with which it roosts at night; many birds pair off monogamously within the larger group, and they are most frequently seen flying in pairs.<ref name="bristolzoo">{{cite web|url=http://www.bristolzoo.org.uk/learning/animals/birds/amazon-parrot |title=Ecuadorian red-lored Amazon |access-date=10 November 2008 |publisher=[[Bristol Zoo]] |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090101174431/http://www.bristolzoo.org.uk/learning/animals/birds/amazon-parrot |archive-date= 1 January 2009 }}</ref>

=== Diet ===
The diet of the lilacine amazon consists primarily of [[fruit]]s, [[Nut (fruit)|nut]]s, [[berry|berries]], and [[seed]]s. Like most parrots, it possesses a powerful [[beak]]; its dexterous [[tongue]] also helps to break down and consume a diverse suite of foodstuffs.<ref name="bristolzoo" />

=== Breeding ===
Sexual maturity is reached at three or four years of age. Like most parrots, the lilacine amazon is a [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous]] breeder and a [[cavity nester]]. The hen will lay two to four eggs in a [[tree]] cavity and incubate them for a period of three weeks; the young [[fledge]] about two months after hatching.<ref name="brough" /><ref name="bristolzoo" />

As with many other parrots, the male will keep the female and chicks nourished during their time in the nest by consuming additional food and [[Regurgitation (digestion)|regurgitating]] it for them.<ref name="brough" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
The lilacine amazon is native to the tropical dry forests of western [[Ecuador]] north of the [[Gulf of Guayaquil]], extending to [[Nariño Department|Nariño]] in extreme south-western [[Colombia]] adjacent to the Ecuadorian border, where it intersects with the subspecies ''A. a. salvini''.<ref name="juniper" />

== Aviculture ==
Lilacine amazons have become well regarded as [[companion parrots]], intelligent with a personality often described as gentle, affectionate, and loyal.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://abcbirds.org/bird/lilacine-amazon/|title=Lilacine Amazon}}</ref> In [[captivity (animal)|captivity]], they enjoy the company of people, and will often form a strong bond with their owner. They are not known as exceptional [[Talking bird|talkers]], but most individuals are able to learn to mimic at least a few phrases.<ref name="brough" /> The [[World Parrot Trust]] recommends that the lilacine amazon be housed in an enclosure with a minimum length of 3 metres.<ref name="worldparrottrust" />

The lilacine amazon has only recently become popular and common in the [[pet]] trade, formerly being quite rare.<ref name="opa" /> At least one [[captive breeding]] program has been established in [[Europe]] to increase the bird's numbers.<ref name="bristolzoo" />
